parsing_res_list.block_content: The 69th William Lowell Putnam Mathematical Competition  Saturday, December 6, 2008

A1 Let $f\colon\ensuremath{\mathbb{R}}^{2}\to$ R be a function such that $f(x,y)+f(y,z)+$ $f(z,x)=0$ for all real numbers , y, and z. Prove that there exists a function $g:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ such that $f(x,y)=$ $g(x)-g(y)$ for all real numbers x and y.

A2 Alanand Bra laa gamei which theytake turns filling entriesof an initially empty $2008\times2008$ array.Alan plays firs.Ateach turn,a playerchooses areal number and places it in a vacant entry. The game ends when all the entries are filled. Alan wins if the determinant of the resulting matrix is nonzero; Barbara wins if it is zero. Which player has a winning strate?

A3 Start with a finite sequence $a_{1},a_{2},\ldots,a_{n}$ of positive integers. If possible, choose two indices $j<k$ : such that $a_{j}$ does not divide $a_{k}$ , and replace $a_{j}$ and $a_{k}by\gcd(a_{j},a_{k})$ and lcm $\mathfrak{n}(a_{j},a_{k})$ , respectively. Prove that if this process is repeated,it musteventuallystopandthe finalsequence does not depend on the choices made. (Note:gcd means greatest common divisor and lcm means least common multiple.)

A5 Let $n\geq3$ be an integer. Let $f(x)$ and $g(x)$ be polynomials with real coeficients such that the points $(f(1),g(1)),(f(2),g(2)),\ldots,(f(n),g(n))$ in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ are the vertices of a regular n-gon in counterclockwise order.Prove that at least oneof $f(x)$ and $g(x)$ has degree greater than or equal to $n-1$

A6 Prove that there exists a constant $c>0$ such that in every nontrivialfinitegroupGthere exists  sequence of length at mostlogG with thepropertythateach element of G equals the product of some subsequence.

(The elements of G in the sequence are not required to be distinct. A subsequence of a sequence is obtained by selecting some of the terms, not necessarily consecutive, without reordering them; for example,$\stackrel{\cdot}{4},4,2$ is a subsequence of 2,4,6,4,2, but 2,2,4 is not.)

B1 What is the maximum number of rational points that can lie on a circle in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ whose center is not a rational point?(A rational point is a point both of whose coordinates are rational numbers.)
B2 Let $F_{0}(x)=\ln x$ : For $n\geq0$ and $x>0$ ,let $F_{n+1}(x)=$ $\scriptstyle\int_{0}^{x}F_{n}\left(t\right){\widehat{d t}}$ . Evaluate
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{n!F_n(1)}{\ln n}B3 What is the largest possible radius of a circle contained

$\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{n!F_n(1)}{\ln n}$ 

B3 What is the largest possible radius of a circle contained 
in a 4-dimensional hypercube of side length 1?

B4 Let p be a prime number. Let $h(x)$  be a polynomial with integer coeficients such that $\widehat{h(0)},\widehat{h(1)},\ldots,\widehat{h(p^2-1)}$ are distinct modulo $p^{2}$ .Show that $\widehat{h(0)},\widehat{h(1)},\ldots,h(p^3-$ 1) are distinct modulo $p^{3}$ .

B5 Find all continuously differentiable functions $f\colon\ensuremath{\mathbb{R}}\to$ R such that for every rational number $q,$ ,the number $f(q)$ is rational and has the same denominator as $q.$ (The denominator of a rational number q is the unique positive integer b such that $q{=}a{\textbar}b$ for some integer a with gcd $\mathrm{l}(a,b)=1.$ (Note: gcd means greatest common divisor.)

B6 Let n and k be positive integers. Say that a permutation σof $\{1,2,\ldots,n\}$ is k-limited if $|\overset{\frown}{\boldsymbol{\upsigma}}(i)-i|\leq k$ for all i. Prove that the number of k-limited permutations of $\{1,2,\ldots,n\}$ · is odd if and only if $n\equiv0\mathrm{~or}1\pmod{2k+1}$ ).

core_content.markdown:
# The 69th William Lowell Putnam Mathematical Competition  Saturday, December 6, 2008

A1 Let $f\colon\ensuremath{\mathbb{R}}^{2}\to$ R be a function such that $f(x,y)+f(y,z)+$ $f(z,x)=0$ for all real numbers , y, and z. Prove that there exists a function $g:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ such that $f(x,y)=$ $g(x)-g(y)$ for all real numbers x and y.

A2 Alanand Bra laa gamei which theytake turns filling entriesof an initially empty $2008\times2008$ array.Alan plays firs.Ateach turn,a playerchooses areal number and places it in a vacant entry. The game ends when all the entries are filled. Alan wins if the determinant of the resulting matrix is nonzero; Barbara wins if it is zero. Which player has a winning strate?

A3 Start with a finite sequence $a_{1},a_{2},\ldots,a_{n}$ of positive integers. If possible, choose two indices $j<k$ : such that $a_{j}$ does not divide $a_{k}$ , and replace $a_{j}$ and $a_{k}by\gcd(a_{j},a_{k})$ and lcm $\mathfrak{n}(a_{j},a_{k})$ , respectively. Prove that if this process is repeated,it musteventuallystopandthe finalsequence does not depend on the choices made. (Note:gcd means greatest common divisor and lcm means least common multiple.)



A4 Define $f\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ by 

$$f(x)=\begin{cases}x&if x\leq e\\xf(\ln x)&if x>e.\end{cases}$$

Does $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}{\frac{1}{f(n)}}$ converge?

A5 Let $n\geq3$ be an integer. Let $f(x)$ and $g(x)$ be polynomials with real coeficients such that the points $(f(1),g(1)),(f(2),g(2)),\ldots,(f(n),g(n))$ in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ are the vertices of a regular n-gon in counterclockwise order.Prove that at least oneof $f(x)$ and $g(x)$ has degree greater than or equal to $n-1$ 



A6 Prove that there exists a constant $c>0$ such that in every nontrivialfinitegroupGthere exists  sequence of length at mostlogG with thepropertythateach element of G equals the product of some subsequence.

(The elements of G in the sequence are not required to be distinct. A subsequence of a sequence is obtained by selecting some of the terms, not necessarily consecutive, without reordering them; for example,$\stackrel{\cdot}{4},4,2$ is a subsequence of 2,4,6,4,2, but 2,2,4 is not.)

B1 What is the maximum number of rational points that can lie on a circle in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ whose center is not a rational point?(A rational point is a point both of whose coordinates are rational numbers.)



B2 Let $F_{0}(x)=\ln x$ : For $n\geq0$ and $x>0$ ,let $F_{n+1}(x)=$ $\scriptstyle\int_{0}^{x}F_{n}\left(t\right){\widehat{d t}}$ . Evaluate 



$$\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{n!F_n(1)}{\ln n}B3 What is the largest possible radius of a circle contained $$



$\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{n!F_n(1)}{\ln n}$ 

B3 What is the largest possible radius of a circle contained 

in a 4-dimensional hypercube of side length 1?



B4 Let p be a prime number. Let $h(x)$  be a polynomial with integer coeficients such that $\widehat{h(0)},\widehat{h(1)},\ldots,\widehat{h(p^2-1)}$ are distinct modulo $p^{2}$ .Show that $\widehat{h(0)},\widehat{h(1)},\ldots,h(p^3-$ 1) are distinct modulo $p^{3}$ .



B5 Find all continuously differentiable functions $f\colon\ensuremath{\mathbb{R}}\to$ R such that for every rational number $q,$ ,the number $f(q)$ is rational and has the same denominator as $q.$ (The denominator of a rational number q is the unique positive integer b such that $q{=}a{\textbar}b$ for some integer a with gcd $\mathrm{l}(a,b)=1.$ (Note: gcd means greatest common divisor.)



B6 Let n and k be positive integers. Say that a permutation σof $\{1,2,\ldots,n\}$ is k-limited if $|\overset{\frown}{\boldsymbol{\upsigma}}(i)-i|\leq k$ for all i. Prove that the number of k-limited permutations of $\{1,2,\ldots,n\}$ · is odd if and only if $n\equiv0\mathrm{~or}1\pmod{2k+1}$ ).
